---
title: "Agentic AI for automating real-world evidence studies"
description: "What actually works when you point coding agents at observational study delivery — where automation helps, where it doesn't, and what has to stay human."
date: 2026-07-18
date-modified: 2026-07-18
categories: [real-world evidence, agentic AI, automation]
author: "Sam Hillman"
draft: true
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <script type="application/ld+json">
      {
        "@context": "https://schema.org",
        "@type": "Article",
        "headline": "Agentic AI for automating real-world evidence studies",
        "description": "What actually works when you point coding agents at observational study delivery.",
        "datePublished": "2026-07-18",
        "author": {
          "@type": "Person",
          "name": "Sam Hillman",
          "url": "https://samhillman.github.io"
        }
      }
      </script>
---

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
**Draft — skeleton only.** Remove `draft: true` from the front matter to publish.
:::

<!--
STRUCTURE NOTE (delete before publishing)

The research on AI-citability suggests: open each H2 with a direct, self-contained answer of
roughly 40-180 words before elaborating. Question-shaped headings get quoted more often.
Concrete numbers and named tools beat abstractions.
-->

## What does an agentic RWE pipeline actually do?

*Answer-first paragraph goes here — two or three sentences that would stand alone if someone
quoted just this bit. Something like: an agentic RWE pipeline decomposes study delivery into
discrete, checkable steps — cohort definition, phenotyping, endpoint derivation, analysis,
QC — and hands each to an agent with tightly scoped tools and a spec to validate against.*

Points to cover:

- The shape of the pipeline I co-designed: what the stages are, what hands off to what.
- Where the specification lives, and why that matters more than the model choice.
- What the agent is *not* allowed to touch.

## Where does automation genuinely help?

- Standardising phenotype and endpoint definitions across studies — the boring, repeated work
  where inconsistency is the real cost.
- Scaffolding analysis code from a protocol, rather than writing it from scratch each time.
- QC and reconciliation: agents are good at checking that outputs match a spec.
- Turnaround time on ad-hoc requests from medical teams.

## Where does it fall down?

- Study design decisions — the judgement calls about confounding, index dates, and eligibility
  that determine whether the answer is right.
- Data quirks that aren't documented anywhere and only a human who has worked the dataset
  knows about.
- Anything where being confidently wrong is expensive: regulatory submissions, external
  control arms.

## What has to stay human?

*The accountability argument. Reviewers and regulators need a named person who can defend the
design. Automation moves where the human attention goes; it doesn't remove the need for it.*

## Practical setup

- Tooling: what I actually use day to day.
- How specs and protocols are written so an agent can act on them.
- Guardrails: version control, deterministic re-runs, freezing outputs, review gates.

## What I'd tell a team starting this

*Closing section — three or four concrete recommendations.*
